verses.text: 38:1-20 This section reports on building the courtyard (38:9-20) and its equipment, including the altar of burnt offering (38:1-7) and the washbasin (38:8).

38:1-20 This section reports on building the courtyard (38:9-20) and its equipment, including the altar of burnt offering (38:1-7) and the washbasin (38:8).

38:8 While the plans for the washbasin (30:17-21) were included in the instructions to the priests (29:38–30:38), here its construction is reported in logical sequence. • This bronze, which could be polished to a mirror-like finish, was probably of especially high quality, as was fitting for use in God’s Tabernacle.

38:9 the court—It occupied a space of one hundred and fifty feet by seventy-five, and it was enclosed by curtains of fine linen about eight feet high, suspended on brazen or copper pillars. Those curtains were secured by rods fastened to the top, and kept extended by being fastened to pins stuck in the ground.

38:10 hooks—The hooks of the pillars in the court were for hanging up the carcasses of the sacrificial beasts—those on the pillars at the entry of the tabernacle were for hanging the sacerdotal robes and other things used in the service.
38:11 sockets—mortices or holes in which the end of the pillars stood.

38:12 For the west side were hangings of fifty cubits: The tabernacle court's western boundary completes the rectangle (50×100 cubits). The careful counting of pillars (ten) and silver fittings underlines the precision the Lord required. No detail of the sanctuary was left to improvisation — it was patterned after the heavenly original (cp. 25:9; Heb 8:5).

38:13 For the east side eastward fifty cubits: The eastern boundary, fronting the rising sun, faced Israel's approach to worship. Throughout Scripture the east entrance carries weight: the cherubim guard the way from the east (Gen 3:24); Ezekiel sees the glory return from the east (Ezek 43:1-4).

38:14 The hangings of the one side of the gate were fifteen cubits: The gate's southern flank, with three pillars and three sockets, holds the curtain that frames the entrance. The proportions (15 + 20 + 15 = 50 cubits across the east face) preserve a measured symmetry that befits the tabernacle's purpose as model of cosmic order (cp. 25:40).

38:15 For the other side of the court gate: The northern flank mirrors the southern (38:14), confirming the gate's twenty-cubit central span. The mirrored measurements were not architectural ornament but spiritual instruction: the way into God's presence is balanced, intentional, and precisely as the Lord prescribed (cp. Heb 9:6-7).

38:16 All the hangings of the court round about were of fine twined linen: *Shesh moshzar* — six-stranded white linen — encloses the entire courtyard. The white wall set apart sacred ground from common space, visible from a distance as Israel encamped tribally around the tabernacle. White figures the holiness that frames God's dwelling (cp. Lev 16:4; Rev 19:8).
38:17 The hooks - and their fillets - The capitals, and the silver bands that went round them; see Clarke's note on Exo 26:32.

38:18 the height in the breadth—or, "in the measure." The sense is that the hangings of the court gate, which was twenty cubits wide, were of the same height as the hangings all round the court [WALL].

38:19 Their pillars were four, and their sockets of brass four: The gate proper — twenty cubits wide — rested on four bronze sockets supporting four pillars draped with the embroidered curtain (38:18). Bronze marks the courtyard's outer ring; silver and gold belong to the inner sanctities. The metallic gradation traces a topography of holiness (cp. Heb 9:1-5).

38:20 All the pins of the tabernacle were of brass: Tent pegs were not afterthoughts. Bronze pins anchored the courtyard's linen wall and the tabernacle's covering, binding the sanctuary to the earth itself. Even the most humble fixture was named, counted, and consecrated — no part of the dwelling was outside the Lord's reckoning (cp. 27:19).

38:21-29 The immense amount of metal reported in this inventory of materials (more than a ton of gold, almost four tons of silver, and two and a half tons of bronze) reflects the Egyptians’ eagerness to get the Israelites out of Egypt, giving the Israelites anything they asked for in order to hurry them on their way (see 12:35-36). 38:21 Since the Tabernacle contained the Ark of the Covenant, it is called the Tabernacle of the Covenant.

38:26 For the earlier discussion of this tax, see 30:11-16. The sanctuary shekel was evidently the standard against which other weights were measured for accuracy. • 603,550: See study note on 12:37.

38:27 And the brass of the offering was seventy talents, and two thousand four hundred shekels. Which, according to Paris weight, were 6177 pounds, ten ounces, two drachms, and sixty four grains (w): this might be had from the neighbouring mountains of Arabia, where it is said (x) the metals of brass and iron were first dug. (w) Vid. Scheuchzer, ut supra. (Physic. Sacr. vol. 2. p. 253.) (x) Aristeas de 72. interpret. p. 42. Vid. Hieron. de loc. Hebr. fol.

38:28 And therewith he made the sockets to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation,.... Which were five; see Exo 26:37. and the brazen altar, and the brazen grate for it, and all the vessels of the altar; which were all made of brass; see Exo 27:2.

38:29 And the sockets of the court round about, and the sockets of the court gate,.... These were also of brass, in all sixty: and all the pins of the tabernacle, and all the pins of the court round about; the pins for the curtains of the tabernacle, and for the hangings of the court; see Exo 27:19. Next: Exodus Chapter 39

38:30 Therewith he made the sockets to the door of the tabernacle: Bezalel's bronze inventory includes door sockets, the great altar, its grate, and all altar vessels. The materials donated by the people (38:29) become the very furniture of worship — gift transformed into ministry (cp. 1 Chr 29:14; 1 Cor 12:5).

38:31 Sockets of the court round about, and of the court gate, and all the pins: The closing inventory completes the tally. Every bronze item is accounted for; every contribution from Israel finds its place in the sanctuary. The exhaustive enumeration testifies that the tabernacle was built with full integrity, the people's gifts neither lost nor diverted.
